gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of efficient recombinant protein secretion in Bacillus subtilis. Activity-based screening methods are limited because not all proteins have detectable enzymatic activity. The goal is to develop an activity-independent, high-throughput monitoring system that simultaneously measures secreted protein levels and cellular secretion stress. The hypothesis is that combining a secretion stress biosensor (mCherry under CssRS control) with an in vivo split GFP assay (GFP11-tagged target protein complemented by added GFP1-10) enables parallel online monitoring, facilitating optimization of signal peptides for high secretion with low stress.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of a combined online monitoring system: an in vivo split GFP assay for activity-independent detection and an mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor. The biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 was constructed by replacing htrA with mCherry. Cutinase Cut11 was used as model. The split GFP assay correlated with enzymatic activity. Screening a signal peptide library identified variants with improved secretion and low stress.
process_signals: Problem: activity-based screening not universally applicable.
Solution: combined split GFP and stress biosensor.
Key result: correlation between split GFP and activity; identification of optimal signal peptides.
methods: In vivo split GFP assay (GFP11 tag, GFP1-10 complementation)
Secretion stress biosensor (CssRS system, mCherry reporter)

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: B. subtilis is a major industrial host for protein secretion, but optimal signal peptide selection is empirical. Activity-based screening is limited. The split GFP assay was previously adapted for in vitro detection of secreted proteins. Secretion stress biosensors based on CssRS system exist. This study combines both to enable simultaneous monitoring of secretion and stress, using cutinase Cut11 as model.

failures_or_limits: Activity-based screening not available for all proteins.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study aims to overcome the limitation of activity-based screening by developing a dual-reporter system that simultaneously monitors secretion yield (split GFP) and cellular stress (mCherry).
The hypothesis is that this combined system can identify signal peptides that maximize secretion while minimizing stress, enabling more efficient recombinant protein production.
The use of a model enzyme (Cut11) allows validation of the split GFP assay against enzymatic activity, establishing correlation.
Screening a signal peptide library with the dual system will reveal relationships between secretion stress and productivity, potentially identifying bottlenecks.
uncertainties_to_resolve: The paper does not explicitly discuss failures or iterative improvements; the trajectory appears linear.
Generalizability to other proteins beyond Cut11 is not addressed in these sections.
Potential limitations of the split GFP assay (e.g., GFP1-10 stability, urea effect) are not detailed.
The screening conditions (MTP vs. BioLector) may affect comparability.
The coverage of the signal peptide library (>99%) assumes random sampling, but biases may exist.

events.summary: Activity-based screening methods are not universally applicable for detecting secreted proteins.
Combining a split GFP assay with a secretion stress biosensor enables activity-independent, high-throughput monitoring of secretion and stress.
The secretion stress biosensor uses the CssRS system to upregulate mCherry in the htrA locus, providing a fluorescence readout of stress.
The biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 was constructed by replacing htrA with mCherry using CRISPR/Cas9.
The split GFP-based detection assay showed good correlation between the amount of secreted cutinase and enzymatic activity.
A signal peptide library of 173 B. subtilis signal peptides fused to cutinase was screened for secretion and stress response.

events.summary: Construction of secretion stress biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 by replacing htrA with mCherry using CRISPR/Cas9.
Construction of Cut11 signal peptide library with 173 Sec signal peptides fused to cut11.
Online monitoring of secretion stress for Cut11 fused to three signal peptides (SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn) in B. subtilis PAL5 using BioLector.
No correlation observed between enzymatic activity and secretion stress for the three signal peptides.
In vivo split GFP assay for online detection of secreted Cut11 by adding GFP1-10 to culture broth during cultivation.
Good correlation between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activity for Cut11 secretion.
The relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme remains to be elucidated.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers key experimental design, stress monitoring, split GFP validation, and initial analysis of signal peptide effects.

events.summary: Good correlation observed between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activity, demonstrating applicability of in vivo split GFP assay for online detection.
Evaluated simultaneous use of mCherry secretion stress biosensor and split GFP assay by varying spacer length to modulate Cut11 secretion.
Good correlation found between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activities (r=0.85, R²=0.73), but sensitivity decreased for low-performing variants.
Enzymatic activities and stress responses showed good correlation for SP_Epr and SP_Bsn (r=0.92, r=0.96), but slightly worse for SP_Pel (r=0.84).
Disproportionally high increase in secretion stress for SP_Pel with spacer ≥7 nt may indicate improper protein folding, reducing efficient secretion.
Screened 480 clones from SP-Cut11 library using split GFP assay and secretion stress biosensor; correlation between split GFP and activity (r=0.77, R²=0.61) but no correlation with stress.
Five top clones recultivated as triplicates; identified signal peptides SP_NamZ, SP_Mpr, SP_DacB; SP_DacB showed higher stress despite similar secretion.
Secretion stress only partially reflects secretion levels; protein misfolding competes with secretion, causing high stress and low secretion.

evidence_bank.quote: However, not every protein can be detected by activity-based screening.
We therefore developed a combined online monitoring system, consisting of an in vivo split GFP assay for activity-independent target detection and an mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor.

The secretion stress biosensor makes use of the CssRS two component quality control system, which upregulates expression of mCherry in the htrA locus thereby allowing a fluorescence readout of secretion stress.
The biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 was successfully constructed by exchanging the protease encoding gene htrA with mCherry via CRISPR/Cas9.
Using the split GFP-based detection assay, we demonstrated a good correlation between the amount of secreted cutinase and the enzymatic activity.
a library of 173 different B. subtilis signal peptides fused to the cutinase was screened for enzymatically active secreted protein and secretion stress response
the genomic htrA gene was replaced by the mCherry gene

Cas9 produced a double-strand break in the htrA locus due to the previously cloned spacer sequence, which was repaired by homologous recombination between the genome and the plasmid-encoded homologous recombination cassette
about 2000 single E. coli DH5α colonies were washed off from agar plates and plasmids were isolated to generate a library containing all 173 different signal peptides
All cutinase producing cultures showed similar growth behavior (Fig. 1A, inset), which allowed a direct comparison of mCherry fluorescence intensities
a correlation between the enzymatic activity and secretion stress was not observed
we tested the addition of GFP1-10 to the culture broth during cultivation thereby enabling in vivo holo-GFP reconstitution
GFP1-10 solution was added directly to the culture broth at a final concentration of 3% (v/v) after 16 h of cultivation
A good correlation was observed between split GFP fluorescence and the corresponding enzymatic activity determined with the substrate pNPP
the relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme or extracellular enzymatic activity remains to be elucidated in more detail
A good correlation was observed between split GFP fluorescence and the corresponding enzymatic activity determined with the substrate pNPP (Fig. 2B).
The results therefore clearly demonstrate the applicability of the in vivo split GFP assay as an easy-to-perform and time-saving in vivo assay suitable for online detection of secreted enzymes.
we evaluated the simultaneous use of the mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 and the in vivo split GFP assay
the sensitivity of the in vivo split GFP assay decreased with inferior performing variants
the enzymatic activities and the stress responses of B. subtilis strains secreting cutinase fused to different signal peptides and with varying spacer lengths showed a good correlation
for spacer lengths≥7 nt the increase of secretion stress response was higher than the increase in cutinase amount and enzymatic activity
The disproportionally high increase in secretion stress may indicate improper protein folding at a significant rate, thereby reducing efficient protein secretion.
480 individual clones were analyzed with respect to the amount and activity of the secreted cutinase and the accompanying secretion stress
no correlation was observed between the secretion stress response and the amount/activity of the secreted cutinase
we selected five clones with the highest level of cutinase secretion based on the split GFP measurement that were subsequently recultivated as biological triplicates
the secretion stress response only partially reflects secretion levels
Protein misfolding competes with effective secretion leading to high-level stress and disproportionately low protein secretion.
It is interesting to note that $SP_{NamZ^{*}}$ -cutinase harbors the point mutation A252S in the GFP11 tag, which may affect holo-GFP reconstitution.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Activity-based screening methods are not universally applicable for detecting secreted proteins, limiting high-throughput optimization of recombinant protein secretion in Bacillus subtilis.

canonical_events.summary: Activity-based screening methods are not universally applicable for detecting secreted proteins.
Combining a split GFP assay with a secretion stress biosensor enables activity-independent, high-throughput monitoring of secretion and stress.
Design of secretion stress biosensor using CssRS system to upregulate mCherry in the htrA locus.
Construction of secretion stress biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 by replacing htrA with mCherry using CRISPR/Cas9.
Construction of Cut11 signal peptide library with 173 Sec signal peptides fused to cutinase.
Online monitoring of secretion stress for Cut11 fused to three signal peptides (SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn) in B. subtilis PAL5 using BioLector.
No correlation observed between enzymatic activity and secretion stress for the three signal peptides.
In vivo split GFP assay for online detection of secreted Cut11 by adding GFP1-10 to culture broth during cultivation.
Good correlation between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activity for Cut11 secretion.
Evaluated simultaneous use of mCherry secretion stress biosensor and split GFP assay by varying spacer length to modulate Cut11 secretion.
Good correlation found between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activities (r=0.85, R²=0.73), but sensitivity decreased for low-performing variants.
Enzymatic activities and stress responses showed good correlation for SP_Epr and SP_Bsn (r=0.92, r=0.96), but slightly worse for SP_Pel (r=0.84).
Disproportionally high increase in secretion stress for SP_Pel with spacer ≥7 nt may indicate improper protein folding, reducing efficient secretion.
Screened 480 clones from SP-Cut11 library using split GFP assay and secretion stress biosensor; correlation between split GFP and activity (r=0.77, R²=0.61) but no correlation with stress.
Five top clones recultivated as triplicates; identified signal peptides SP_NamZ, SP_Mpr, SP_DacB; SP_DacB showed higher stress despite similar secretion.
Secretion stress only partially reflects secretion levels; protein misfolding competes with secretion, causing high stress and low secretion.
The relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme remains to be elucidated.
